title: Bounding the Critique Loop
concept: Setting exit conditions so self-reflection doesn't loop infinitely or over-correct working code.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: why can a model be 'sycophantic' when it critiques its own work?

options.text: It tends to agree with criticism even when its first answer was already right
It refuses to ever change its answer
It can only critique other models, not itself

exercises.explain: Models often 'cave' to critique and flip a correct answer to a wrong one just to seem agreeable. That's why we ask for objective, evidence-based critique instead of vague 'are you sure?' nudges.

cards.title: When does it stop?
cards.body: A model checks its work, fixes it, checks again, fixes again... forever. Without a rule to stop, a critique loop can run until it runs out of time or money.

cards.title: Exit conditions
cards.body: An exit condition is a rule that tells the loop when to stop. Common ones: 'stop after 3 tries', 'stop when the answer passes all checks', or 'stop if no changes were made'.

cards.title: Proofreading a paragraph
cards.body: You reread your essay to catch mistakes. But if you keep 'fixing' good sentences, you make it worse. Smart writers stop once it reads well, or after a couple of passes.

cards.title: A bounded loop
cards.body: Rule: 'Critique and revise up to 3 times. Stop early if the code passes all tests.' On try 2 the tests pass, so the loop exits. No wasted, risky extra edits.

cards.title: Myth: more loops = better
cards.body: Not true! Extra loops can 'over-correct' code that already worked, breaking it. More reflection isn't always more quality.

cards.title: Unbounded vs bounded
cards.body: A stopping rule protects working code and saves resources.

left.text: Loops forever, keeps editing good code until it breaks

right.text: Stops at 3 tries or when tests pass, keeping the good version

exercises.prompt: Which is a good exit condition for a self-critique loop?

options.text: Stop when the answer passes all the checks
Stop only if the user closes the app
Add one more edit every time, no limit

exercises.explain: A clear exit condition like 'passes all checks' lets the loop end at a good answer instead of running endlessly or over-editing.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Running more critique loops always improves the result.
exercises.explain: False. Too many loops can over-correct code that already worked, making it worse. Bounding the loop protects a good answer.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about stopping a loop.
exercises.sentence: A maximum number of tries is one simple exit condition that keeps a loop from running forever.
exercises.explain: An exit condition is the rule that tells the loop when to stop, such as a maximum number of tries.

exercises.prompt: Which reflection rule was written by an AI to safely bound its own critique loop?

options.text: I will revise up to 3 times, and stop immediately once all tests pass or no changes are needed.
I will revise until I feel like it, whenever that happens.
I will revise every line one hundred more times just to be safe.

exercises.explain: The AI-written rule has a clear limit (3 tries) and an early-exit (tests pass), which prevents infinite loops and over-correction.

exercises.prompt: An agent fixes a bug on try 1, and the code now passes every test. What should a well-bounded critique loop do next?

options.text: Stop, because the exit condition 'all tests pass' is met, keeping the working code
Keep looping forever to be extra careful
Delete the working code and start over
Edit random lines to prove it tried hard

exercises.explain: A bounded loop uses exit conditions like a max number of tries and 'stop when checks pass.' Once the code works, stopping protects it from over-correction and wasted effort. Great self-reflection knows not just how to improve, but when to stop.
